seed_nodes.statement: Let T_p : [0,1) -> [0,1) be the map T_p(x) = p*x mod 1 for integer p >= 2. Two classes of T_p-invariant ergodic Borel probability measures are known: Lebesgue measure and uniform measures on finite rational orbits.
T_p-invariance Fourier characterization: a Borel probability measure mu on [0,1) is T_p-invariant if and only if its Fourier-Stieltjes coefficients satisfy mu_hat(n) = mu_hat(p*n) for all n in Z.
Rudolph's Theorem: if mu is ergodic, jointly T_2- and T_3-invariant, and has positive entropy, then mu is Lebesgue measure. The zero-entropy case remains open.
Hausdorff dimension 0 does not imply pure atomicity for a single T_p-invariant measure; the challenge is to use the joint T_2 and T_3 invariance.
The joint T_2/T_3 action is multiplicatively independent since log 2 / log 3 is irrational, strongly constraining any common invariant measure.

Frontier theorem seed: Host's disjointness / rigidity technology for multiplicatively independent maps gives strong restrictions on common invariant structures. It may be used as a black box seed, but it does not by itself identify the exact remaining obstruction in the zero-entropy case.

Frontier partial result seed: in several related rigidity settings, entropy and dimension estimates force strong regularity or atomicity conclusions. Such results may be used as heuristic or black-box guidance, but they do not directly settle the T_2/T_3 zero-entropy case.

target.statement: Furstenberg's Conjecture (zero-entropy case): Any ergodic Borel probability measure on [0,1) that is invariant under both T_2 : x |-> 2x mod 1 and T_3 : x |-> 3x mod 1 and has zero metric entropy h_mu(T_2) = 0 must be purely atomic — that is, it must be the uniform measure on a single finite orbit under the semigroup generated by T_2 and T_3.

planning_constraints: Frontier rigidity theorems such as Host-type disjointness technology may be used as black-box seeds.
You must explicitly identify why those frontier seeds still leave an unresolved zero-entropy gap.
Do not treat the full zero-entropy Furstenberg conjecture itself as an available seed.
